content.text: From Single Agents to Orchestrated Systems

A single LLM with tool access is sufficient for narrow, well-scoped tasks. Enterprise workflows are rarely that simple: a FNOL intake may need to classify the claim type, retrieve policy terms, check fraud indicators, draft a coverage determination, and validate that determination against regulatory requirements — all in one interaction. When a single agent becomes too broad to be reliable, orchestration patterns distribute the work across specialized agents while keeping the overall system coherent.

A reference pattern is a named, reusable agent topology with defined data flows, failure modes, and tradeoffs. Using named patterns in design conversations and ADRs allows architects and engineers to communicate intent without re-describing topology from scratch every time.

Choose the simplest pattern that meets the reliability and scope requirements. Every additional agent in a system multiplies the latency, cost, and debugging surface. Start with a single ReAct agent and add orchestration only where it solves a specific problem.

attrs.alt: Four canonical agent orchestration patterns: ReAct loop, Router, Planner-Executor, and Generator-Critic

content.text: Pattern 1: ReAct Agent (Single Agent with Tools)

The ReAct (Reason + Act) pattern is the building block from which all other patterns are composed. A single LLM receives a task and a set of tool definitions. It reasons about the task, selects a tool, observes the result, reasons again, and repeats until it can produce a final answer.
Attribute
Value
Topology
Single LLM + tool set in a loop
When to use
Self-contained tasks where one model has sufficient context to complete the goal
Maximum tool calls
Set an explicit limit (e.g., 15) to prevent infinite loops
Failure mode
Tool call loops that make no progress; hallucinated tool arguments
Context management
Full history accumulates in context window; summarise at checkpoints for long tasks
Pattern 2: Router (Intent-Based Dispatch)

The router pattern uses a lightweight classifier — often a small LLM or a fine-tuned intent model — to dispatch incoming requests to the most appropriate specialized agent. Each specialist is smaller, cheaper, and more reliable within its domain than a single general-purpose agent trying to handle everything.
Attribute
Value
Topology
Classifier → N specialist agents → optional merger
When to use
When incoming requests span clearly distinct domains (billing vs. claims vs. technical support)
Classification options
LLM-based (zero-shot), embedding-based nearest-neighbour, keyword rules, fine-tuned classifier
Failure mode
Misclassification sends a request to the wrong specialist; classifier must be evaluated on a representative query set
Latency
Adds one classifier round-trip; specialist runs in parallel if multi-label routing is needed
Pattern 3: Planner-Executor

The planner-executor pattern separates strategic decomposition from tactical execution. A planner LLM receives the high-level goal and produces a structured plan — an ordered list of steps with inputs, outputs, and dependencies. Executor agents then carry out each step, potentially in parallel where dependencies allow.

This pattern is suited for complex, multi-step workflows that benefit from a deliberate planning phase. The planner can revise the plan if an executor returns an unexpected result before committing downstream steps.
Attribute
Value
Topology
Planner LLM → task graph → N executor agents → synthesizer
When to use
Multi-step workflows with dependencies; tasks requiring parallel execution of independent subtasks
Plan representation
Ordered list, DAG, or JSON task graph with explicit dependency edges
Failure mode
Planner produces infeasible plans; executor failures cascade if no replanning step
Replanning
Add a replanning hook: if an executor fails, the planner sees the failure and revises remaining steps
Pattern 4: Generator-Critic (Reflection Loop)

The generator-critic pattern uses two agents in opposition: a generator produces a candidate response, and a critic evaluates it against explicit criteria. If the critic finds deficiencies, the generator revises until the critic approves or a maximum iteration count is reached.

This pattern is particularly effective for high-stakes outputs — regulatory documents, coverage determinations, API contracts — where a single-pass generation is not reliable enough. The critic can be prompted with a checklist: completeness, factual grounding, regulatory compliance, or style conformance.
Attribute
Value
Topology
Generator → Critic → conditional loop back to Generator
When to use
High-stakes outputs requiring verifiable quality; outputs with explicit acceptance criteria
Critic design
Give the critic a structured rubric with pass/fail criteria and required evidence, not just subjective feedback
Termination
Max revisions (e.g., 3) to prevent infinite loops; escalate to human review if no pass after max
Cost
Each revision adds generator + critic LLM calls; use a cheaper generator and reserve the expensive critic model

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class CriticFeedback:
    passed: bool
    score: float                # 0.0 to 1.0
    issues: list[str]
    required_revisions: list[str]

def generate(task: str, previous_feedback: Optional[CriticFeedback], llm_client) -> str:
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": task}]
    if previous_feedback:
        revision_prompt = (
            f"Your previous draft had these issues:\n"
            + "\n".join(f"- {i}" for i in previous_feedback.issues)
            + "\n\nRequired changes:\n"
            + "\n".join(f"- {r}" for r in previous_feedback.required_revisions)
            + "\n\nPlease produce a revised draft addressing all issues."
        )
        messages.append({"role": "user", "content": revision_prompt})
    return llm_client.complete(messages=messages).content[0].text

def critique(draft: str, criteria: list[str], llm_client) -> CriticFeedback:
    import json
    criteria_text = "\n".join(f"- {c}" for c in criteria)
    prompt = (
        f"Evaluate this draft against the following criteria:\n{criteria_text}\n\n"
        f"Draft:\n{draft}\n\n"
        'Return JSON: {"passed": bool, "score": float, "issues": [], "required_revisions": []}'
    )
    response = llm_client.complete(messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}])
    data = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    return CriticFeedback(**data)

def generator_critic(task: str, criteria: list[str], llm_client, max_revisions: int = 3) -> tuple[str, int]:
    feedback: Optional[CriticFeedback] = None
    for revision in range(max_revisions + 1):
        draft = generate(task, feedback, llm_client)
        feedback = critique(draft, criteria, llm_client)
        if feedback.passed:
            return draft, revision
    # Final draft even if critic not satisfied — escalate externally
    return draft, max_revisions
Combining Patterns
Real enterprise systems compose these patterns. A common combination for insurance claim processing:
Router:
Classify the claim type (auto, property, liability) and route to the appropriate specialist pipeline.
Planner-Executor:
The specialist pipeline plans the claim workflow (retrieve policy, check exclusions, calculate coverage, draft determination) and executes steps in dependency order.
ReAct:
Each executor step is itself a ReAct agent with domain-specific tools (policy retrieval, actuarial lookup, fraud scoring API).
Generator-Critic:
The final coverage determination letter passes through a critic that checks regulatory language requirements and citation accuracy before delivery.

When composing patterns, define clear contracts between layers: what each sub-agent accepts as input, what it returns, and what errors it raises. Without explicit contracts, debugging becomes extremely difficult when a multi-agent chain produces wrong output.
Pattern Selection Guide
Situation
Recommended Pattern
Reason
Single domain, <15 tool calls expected
ReAct
Simplest; easiest to debug; lowest latency
Requests span 3+ distinct domains with different tools
Router → ReAct per domain
Specialist agents outperform generalists within narrow domains
Task requires 5+ sequential steps with parallelism
Planner-Executor
Explicit plan enables parallel step execution and replanning
Output has verifiable quality criteria (compliance, style, accuracy)
Generator-Critic
Structured critique catches failures a single-pass generation misses
Research or analysis task requiring multiple information gathering passes
ReAct with summarise-and-continue
Single agent with context summarisation avoids coordination overhead
High-stakes output that also has multi-step prerequisites
Planner-Executor + Generator-Critic for final step
Composing patterns applies them where each adds value
Operational Considerations
Observability Across Agent Hops

In a multi-agent system, a trace must span all agents in the call chain to be useful. Each agent call should emit a span with its input, output, model, token counts, latency, and tool calls. A correlation ID propagated from the initial user request links all spans into a single distributed trace.

import uuid
from contextlib import contextmanager
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class AgentSpan:
    trace_id: str
    span_id: str
    parent_span_id: str | None
    agent_name: str
    input_tokens: int = 0
    output_tokens: int = 0
    tool_calls: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    latency_ms: float = 0.0

@contextmanager
def agent_span(trace_id: str, agent_name: str, parent_span_id: str | None = None):
    import time
    span = AgentSpan(
        trace_id=trace_id,
        span_id=str(uuid.uuid4()),
        parent_span_id=parent_span_id,
        agent_name=agent_name,
    )
    start = time.monotonic()
    try:
        yield span
    finally:
        span.latency_ms = (time.monotonic() - start) * 1000
        emit_span(span)  # send to APM / OTEL collector

def emit_span(span: AgentSpan) -> None:
    # Implementation: write to OpenTelemetry, Datadog, Langfuse, etc.
    pass
Cost Guardrails
Multi-agent pipelines can spend tokens far faster than single-agent calls. Set explicit token budgets per request and per-pipeline:
Per-request budget:
Track cumulative tokens across all agents in a single user request and halt if a threshold is exceeded.
Planner efficiency:
Prefer a cheaper model for the planner (GPT-4o-mini, Claude Haiku) unless plan quality is measurably better with a large model.
Critic model tier:
The critic does not need to generate prose — it needs to judge. A mid-tier model with a structured rubric often outperforms an expensive model given an open-ended critique prompt.
Human-in-the-Loop Escalation
Every orchestration pattern needs a defined escalation path for cases the agent system cannot resolve confidently. Configure escalation triggers at:
Maximum iteration count reached without a passing critic score
Tool error rate above threshold in a single ReAct loop
Router classification confidence below the minimum (e.g., 0.6)
Planner-executor step failure with no viable replanning path

An agent that silently delivers a low-confidence answer is more dangerous than one that escalates to a human. Build explicit escalation into every orchestration pattern as a first-class path, not an afterthought.
Summary

The four reference patterns — ReAct, Router, Planner-Executor, and Generator-Critic — cover the majority of enterprise GenAI orchestration needs. Each has a distinct problem it solves well and a characteristic failure mode to guard against:
ReAct:
Best for self-contained, tool-using tasks. Guard against infinite loops with explicit iteration limits.
Router:
Best when request types are clearly distinct. Guard against misclassification with confidence thresholds and fallbacks.
Planner-Executor:
Best for multi-step parallel workflows. Guard against cascading failures with replanning hooks and per-step error handling.
Generator-Critic:
Best for high-stakes outputs with verifiable criteria. Guard against runaway revision cycles with maximum iteration counts and human escalation.

Document the chosen pattern in the project ADR. Composing patterns adds power but also adds complexity — justify each composition with a measurable reliability or quality requirement the simpler alternative cannot meet.
